losses_in_past_5_years.losses_past_5_yea.answer: Frozen pipe in second-floor bathroom during an extended cold snap while family was away for a weekend. Pipe burst behind the vanity, resulting in water damage to the bathroom flooring, ceiling of the dining room below, and a section of drywall. Claim included emergency mitigation (fans/dehumidifiers), plumbing repair, replacement of damaged hardwood in dining room, and repainting of affected walls and ceiling. No injuries and no mold growth due to quick response.

losses_in_past_5_years.details_of_loss.answer: Single prior homeowners claim in the last 5 years: on 2023-02-11 a frozen pipe burst behind the second-floor bathroom vanity while the insured and family were away for a long weekend. A neighbor noticed water dripping from an exterior soffit and contacted the insured. Water remediation company responded same day, and the claim covered plumbing repair, removal and replacement of water-damaged drywall, repainting the dining room ceiling and walls, and replacement of a portion of the hardwood flooring that cupped due to moisture. The loss did not involve any liability exposure or injuries, and there have been no repeated water damage incidents since. The insured has since installed smart water leak sensors and keeps the heat at a minimum of 60°F when away in winter.

premises.construction_type.answer: Wood frame construction with vinyl siding and asphalt shingle roof; full basement, typical New England colonial-style single-family home.

premises.plumbing_type.answer: Predominantly copper supply lines with PVC drain lines; PEX used for 2023 bathroom repair after frozen pipe claim.

premises.roof_type.answer: Architectural asphalt shingles, 30-year rated, installed during 2016 roof replacement.

premises.wiring_type.answer: Modern copper wiring with circuit breakers; no aluminum or knob-and-tube present.

premises.heating_type.answer: Natural gas-fired forced hot water baseboard system with two heating zones and programmable thermostats.

additional_information_for_underwriting..answer:
The named insured, Jonathan M. Carter, is a 44-year-old software engineering manager employed full-time by a Boston-based technology company. He has been with his current employer for over 7 years and works primarily from home three days per week, with two days spent in the Boston office. His spouse, Emily Carter, is a part-time occupational therapist who works off-premises at a local rehabilitation center. They have two children, ages 10 and 7, who attend the local public elementary school in Marblehead.

OCCUPANCY AND LIFESTYLE:
The property at 184 Willow Brook Lane is the family’s primary residence and is occupied year-round. Typical daily occupancy includes at least one adult in the home during daytime hours due to Mr. Carter’s hybrid work schedule, which reduces the amount of time the house is left completely unattended. The family does not take extended vacations of more than 2 weeks without arranging for a neighbor or relative to check on the property, bring in mail, and verify that the heating system is functioning, especially during winter months. The insured has provided a spare key and alarm access code to a trusted next-door neighbor for emergency purposes.

PROPERTY MAINTENANCE AND UPKEEP:
Jonathan is attentive to home maintenance. He keeps a spreadsheet of routine tasks such as gutter cleaning (performed each spring and fall), furnace service (annually in October by a licensed HVAC contractor), and chimney inspection (although there is a decorative, non-functional fireplace, the flue and structure are inspected every few years). The exterior siding was power-washed in 2022, and small areas of trim were repainted. The driveway is asphalt and is professionally plowed during significant snow events; Jonathan also shovels walkways promptly to reduce slip-and-fall hazards.

WATER DAMAGE MITIGATION:
Following the 2023 frozen pipe claim, the insured took several steps to reduce future water damage risk. He installed a smart leak detection and automatic water shutoff system on the main water line (Flo by Moen) which is connected to his home Wi-Fi and mobile phone. The system can automatically close the main water valve if a leak is detected or if abnormal water usage is observed. Additionally, inexpensive point leak sensors were placed under sinks, near the water heater, and in the basement near the sump pump crock. The family now keeps the home heated to at least 60°F during cold weather, even when traveling, and leaves interior doors open to promote heat circulation.

ELECTRICAL AND FIRE SAFETY:
The electrical system was updated in 2012, including replacement of the main panel and verification of proper grounding. There are no known aluminum branch circuits or knob-and-tube wiring. Smoke detectors are a combination of hard-wired and battery-backup units located in all bedrooms, hallways, and on each level. Carbon monoxide detectors are installed on both the first and second floors. The family keeps a 5 lb ABC fire extinguisher in the kitchen pantry and another in the basement near the furnace. They do not use space heaters as a primary heat source; on rare occasions, a small UL-listed electric space heater is used in a home office and is turned off and unplugged when not in use.

SECURITY AND LIABILITY CONSIDERATIONS:
The home is protected by a professionally monitored security system provided by a national alarm company. The system includes door and window sensors, glass-break detectors on the first floor, and interior motion detectors. The alarm is armed every night and whenever the home is unoccupied. A video doorbell camera and a backyard camera, both with cloud recording, are also in place. Exterior lighting is on motion sensors at the front and rear of the property to deter unwanted visitors.

There are no pools, hot tubs, or trampolines on the premises. The backyard is modest in size and fully fenced with a 6-foot wooden privacy fence; there is a small play set (swing and slide) installed on a rubber mulch surface. The family enforces rules that neighborhood children must be supervised by an adult when using the play set, and they have posted simple safety rules for their own children and guests.

ANIMALS AND OTHER EXPOSURES:
The Carters own one dog, a 5-year-old golden retriever named Bailey. Bailey is up to date on vaccinations, has no bite history, and is well socialized. The dog is never left unattended outside and is walked on a leash in the neighborhood. There are no other pets, exotic animals, or livestock at the premises. There are no firearms in the home.

BUSINESS AND HOBBIES:
Neither Jonathan nor Emily operates a business from the home that would involve customer foot traffic, inventory storage, or hazardous materials. Jonathan occasionally works overtime from his home office using a company-issued laptop and standard office equipment only. Emily does not see patients at the residence; all of her professional services are provided at the rehabilitation center. Hobbies are low-risk and primarily include running, reading, gardening, and occasional small woodworking projects using portable tools in the basement workshop area. These projects are for personal use only (e.g., building shelves, small furniture pieces) and do not involve commercial sales or significant chemical use.

RENOVATION HISTORY AND FUTURE PLANS:
The house has undergone several updates over the years, but there are no current or planned structural renovations. A kitchen update was completed in 2018 (new cabinets, countertops, and appliances) performed by a licensed contractor and inspected as required. Bathrooms were partially updated in 2020 (new fixtures and tile work). Future plans include repainting interior rooms and replacing some carpeting with hardwood flooring, which are cosmetic in nature and not expected to change the overall risk profile.

INSURANCE HISTORY AND ATTITUDE TOWARD RISK:
Jonathan has carried homeowners insurance continuously since purchasing the property in 2012, with no lapse in coverage. Aside from the 2023 frozen pipe incident, there have been no prior property or liability claims in at least the last 10 years. The family is generally conservative in its approach to risk; they maintain working smoke and CO detectors, practice basic fire safety, and are proactive in addressing small maintenance issues before they become larger problems. They understand their responsibilities as homeowners and are receptive to loss control recommendations from the carrier or agency.

In summary, this is a well-maintained, owner-occupied single-family residence in a stable neighborhood with good protective features, a responsible insured, and no significant unusual exposures beyond ordinary family living.
